patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hey diana it's good to see you in here so i see that you injured your wrist could you tell me a bit about what happened"] yeah i was walking up and down the stairs i was doing my laundry and i slipped and i tried to catch myself and i put my arms out to catch myself and then all of a sudden i just my wrist started to hurt real bad and it got real swollen
[Clinician asked: "wow okay so which wrist are we talking about left or right"] it's my right one of course
[Clinician asked: "okay and then have you ever injured this arm before"] no i have not
[Clinician asked: "okay alright so on a scale of one to ten how severe is the pain"] gosh it's like a nine
[Clinician asked: "wow okay have you done anything to ease it"] yeah i did the ice thing i put ice on it and then i you know i even i have a ace wrap at home i try to do that
[Clinician asked: "mm-hmm"] and then i took some ibuprofen but it helps a little bit but it's just it's it's just not right
[Clinician asked: "okay"] really
[Clinician asked: "yeah okay have you sorry i'm trying to think how long ago did this injury happen"] this happened yesterday morning
[Clinician asked: "okay"] maybe just you know i just bumped it but
[Clinician asked: "okay"] it's just not it's really bad

[Clinician asked: "okay no i understand okay so i'm going so you said you were doing laundry"] yes i had my back hit my basket and for some reason this cold started to kinda fall out a little bit i was trying to catch it i missed a step and i just totally
[Clinician asked: "okay alright any does the pain extend anywhere"] no not really
[Clinician asked: "okay"] it's just really along my wrist
[Clinician asked: "okay any numbness any tingling"] a little one and one ca n't tell if it's just because of the swelling in my wrist but just i can like i can feel it my fingers still
[Clinician asked: "mm-hmm"] but just maybe a little bit of tingling
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and are you so so okay i'm gon na think on this but in the meantime i'm gon na do my physical exam alright"] okay

[Clinician asked: "okay so you know looking at your looking at your head and your neck i do n't appreciate any like adenopathy no thyromegaly no no carotid bruit looking at your listening to your heart i do n't appreciate any murmur no rub no gallop your lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally your lower legs you "] yes
[Clinician asked: "okay"] it does
[Clinician asked: "and what about the outside does that hurt as well"] yeah it does
[Clinician asked: "are you able to move your wrist towards your arm like"] not without extreme pain
[Clinician asked: "okay so pain on flexion what about extension when you pick your wrist up"] yeah i have a hard time doing that actually
[Clinician asked: "alright what about we're gon na go ahead and hold your arm like straight like flat and then try and move it sideways does radial deviation hurt"] yeah
[Clinician asked: "alright and then lateral as well"] yeah it's really hard to move any direction of this hand for some reason
[Clinician asked: "alright so wrist abduction adduction positive for pain on movement are you able to make a fist"] hmmm yeah a little bit but i ca n't do it really tight

[Clinician asked: "okay alright okay so i'm just gon na go ahead and feel on your fingers really quickly alright metacarpals intact noticed some obvious swelling ecchymosis obvious swelling and bruising tenderness on palpation throughout there is evidence of potential fracture feeling some bony crepitus alright so thi"] yeah i would say it kinda goes away when i take that ibuprofen but for the most part i feel it i feel it there and it it's just really really bad when i move it all

[Clinician asked: "okay so when you like is there a position either hurts less or hurts more like say if your arm is raised and elevated over your head does it hurt more or is it just best to keep it like down"] it's good if i keep it a little bit above my like a little i guess a little bit like around my like just a regular level like if you're typing or something and then i just put it on a pillow and i just let it stay straight like i feel better

[Clinician asked: "okay yeah no i do n't think i understand completely okay so i took a look at your vitals and your blood pressure is a little elevated but honestly that's probably to do with the pain right our body can respond to pain in that way we are looking at like a hundred and forty over over seventy it's not "] i'm sorry
[Clinician asked: "pardon what"] a what
[Clinician asked: "we're gon na brace you we're gon na give you a brace"] okay thank you

[Clinician asked: "sorry no problem sorry yeah not a thumb spica we're gon na brace your arm and you're gon na have that we we have a couple of options but i think the best course of action is gon na be for surgery we will in the meantime give you pain medication i wan na put you on fifty milligrams of ultram every si"] when do i have to have the surgery

[Clinician asked: "we would like it to happen as quickly as possible you know your body is a wonderful miracle and it's going to start trying to heal on it's own what we need to do is get your wrist straight and then like put screws in to make sure that we hold it in place or else it could like heal and malform"] okay
[Clinician asked: "alright so what"] how how long do i have to wear that brace
[Clinician asked: "you're gon na be wearing the brace for about six weeks"] six weeks

[Clinician asked: "yeah so you're gon na you're gon na come in for your surgery we're gon na perform it you're gon na stay overnight and then you'll be bracing it for six weeks in the meantime you'll also then go to physical therapy i want you there like we're gon na they're gon na do an assessment and determine how m"] oof

[Clinician asked: "these these deform these deformities that really that really kind of hurt is the short version alright no problem any other questions"] no well i am going on vacation do i need to cancel it like can i still go even with the i mean after the surgery
[Clinician asked: "yeah"] do it as soon as possible i'm going a vacation in a month so
[Clinician asked: "okay how long is the vacation"] it's only for like a couple weeks

[Clinician asked: "okay well so you might have to postpone it just because depending on what physical therapy says right if they feel that you can sustain if you can like sustain the exercises while you're gone that if there's something that you can do by yourself then you should be fine but we do wan na give it you s"] no vacation in a month

[Clinician asked: "okay okay yeah so how about in a month we come you come back let's do a checkup again see where we are at and then we can assess whether or not this is something that i would recommend you do"] that sounds good thank you
[Clinician asked: "no problem bye"] bye
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right wrist injury.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Diana Scott is a pleasant 61-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right wrist injury. The patient sustained this injury yesterday morning, 05/12/2022, when she slipped on the stairs while carrying a laundry basket. She states she tried to catch herself with her arms outstretched. The patient reports an immediate onset of pain and swelling in her right wrist. She denies any previous injuries to her right arm. The patient rates her pain level as a 9/10. Her pain is aggravated by movement. The patient also reports numbness and tingling in her fingers. She has been icing and wrapping her right wrist with an ACE wrap. The patient has also been taking ibuprofen, which provides some relief. She notes fully extending her arm while resting it on a pillow alleviates some of her pain as well.

The patient is going on vacation in 1 month.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right wrist pain and swelling.
Neurological: Reports numbness and tingling to the digits of the right hand.

VITALS

Blood Pressure: Elevated at 140/70 mmHg.
Heart Rate: 80 beats per minute.
Respiratory Rate: 20 breaths per minute.
Body Temperature: 97.2 degrees F.
HEAD: Normocephalic
NECK: No swelling noted
CV: No bilateral lower extremity edema. No carotid bruit. No murmurs, gallops or rubs heard during auscultation of the heart. Palpabale pulses to the bilateral lower extremities.
RESPIRATORY: Normal respiratory effort no respiratory distress. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
GI/GU: Non-distended
BACK: No evidence of trauma or deformity
NECK: No adenopathy. No thyromegaly.
MSK: Examination of the right wrist: Limited range of movement. Tenderness to palpation. Pain on flexion and extension. Pain with radial deviation and lateral deviation. Pain with wrist abduction and adduction. The metacarpals are intact. Obvious swelling and bruising. Tenderness on palpation throughout. There is evidence of potential fracture feeling and bony crepitus.

RESULTS

X-rays of the right wrist were taken today. These reveal the fracture appears extra-articular and proximal to the radioulnar joint. Dorsal angulation of the distal fracture fragment is present to a variable degree. Dorsal angulation is severe, presenting with a dinner fork deformity. An ulnar styloid fracture is present.

ASSESSMENT

Right wrist Colles fracture.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have discussed with the patient that her x-rays revealed a right wrist Colles fracture. We discussed treatment options and I have recommended that we proceed with a right wrist ORIF and all indicated procedures. We reviewed the risks, benefits, and alternatives of the surgery. I explained that we should schedule the operation for as soon as possible to ensure adequate healing and to limit malformation of the wrist. I advised that this procedure will require her to be admitted to the hospital for an overnight stay.

In the meantime, the patient will be placed in a thumb spica brace and receive instructions on its proper usage. I have prescribed the patient Ultram 50 mg every 6 hours to treat her pain. We also discussed that her postoperative course will include wearing a brace for 6 weeks. A few weeks after surgery, the patient will initiate formal physical therapy and will attend 3 times per week to strengthen her right wrist. The patient has a 2-week vacation planned in 1 month. I recommended that we consult with her physical therapist to determine if there are exercises she can perform on her own instead of postponing her vacation.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 4 weeks status post surgery to discuss her progress.

naive.response_text:
I'm really sorry to hear about your wrist injury. It sounds like you're in a lot of pain, and I understand how concerning this must be for you. The treatment plan we've discussed is designed to ensure the best possible recovery, starting with surgery to stabilize your wrist. It's important to follow this plan closely to avoid complications and ensure proper healing.

Regarding your upcoming vacation, we'll keep it in mind as we monitor your progress. Your recovery is our priority, and we'll work with you to make any necessary adjustments. Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns in the meantime. We're here to support you through this.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all the details about your wrist injury, Diana. It sounds like you're in a lot of pain, and I can understand how concerning this must be for you. Suffering from such severe pain and swelling for even a day is tough, and I'm glad you took steps like icing and using the ACE wrap. It’s clear you’re trying hard to manage this, and I want to make sure we get it sorted out for you.

From what you’ve described, the injury happened suddenly while doing laundry, and the pain is pretty intense—right at a nine on the scale. Given the swelling, limited movement, and the tenderness, we need to consider the possibility of a fracture or ligament damage. I can see why you’re worried, and it’s important we address this promptly. We’ll likely need imaging, like an X-ray, to get a clearer picture of what’s going on. In the meantime, keeping your wrist immobilized with a brace should help reduce the pain and prevent further damage.

We’re planning surgery as soon as possible to ensure proper healing, and you’ll need to wear the brace for about six weeks. After that, physical therapy will be crucial to restore movement and strength. I know your upcoming vacation is important, but we’ll need to see how you’re progressing post-surgery before deciding if it’s safe for you to travel. In a month, we’ll reassess and make a plan that works best for your recovery.

Before you leave, can you summarize what we’ve discussed? That way, I can make sure everything’s clear and you feel confident about the next steps. You’re doing the right things already, and with this plan, we’ll get you back on track soon.

framework_only.response_text:
It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of pain with your wrist injury. I understand how frustrating and uncomfortable that must be.

We'll need to schedule a follow-up visit to assess your wrist and determine the next steps for treatment.

In the meantime, continue to rest your wrist, avoid activities that worsen the pain, and take the prescribed pain medication as directed.

Can you tell me in your own words what you understand about the next steps for your wrist injury?

Please call us if your symptoms worsen or if you experience any new issues. We look forward to seeing you at your next appointment.

retrieval_only.response_text:
It sounds like your wrist injury is really bothering you, especially since it's gotten worse over the past day.

Wrist injuries can vary, and it's important to assess the specific symptoms you're experiencing. The pain, swelling, numbness, and tingling you're describing suggest we need to carefully evaluate the injury to determine the best course of action.

We'll schedule an imaging study to get a better look at your wrist and assess the extent of the injury.

Based on the findings, we'll recommend the appropriate treatment, which may include physical therapy or further evaluation if needed.

Can you tell me in your own words what we're planning to do next for your wrist injury?

Let's plan to follow up in a week to review the results of the imaging and discuss your treatment options. If your symptoms worsen or if you experience severe pain, please reach out sooner.

full.response_text:
It sounds like your wrist injury has been getting worse, especially with the pain and swelling you're experiencing.

Given the severity and worsening symptoms of your wrist injury, it's important to assess it thoroughly. The primary step is to evaluate the extent of the injury through imaging, such as an X-ray or MRI, to rule out fractures or ligament tears. This approach is supported by clinical guidelines to ensure appropriate treatment.

We'll schedule an imaging study to get a clearer picture of your wrist injury.

Based on the results, we'll determine the next steps, which may include a referral to an orthopedic specialist or physical therapy.

In the meantime, continue using the ice, ace wrap, and ibuprofen as you've been doing, and avoid activities that worsen the pain.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to follow up in a week to review the imaging results. If the pain becomes unbearable or if there's significant swelling, please call us immediately.
